name: Money & Investing
description: Timeless sense about wealth, markets, and money from investors who built it.
author: Quips Editorial

quotes.content: It's far better to buy a wonderful company at a fair price than a fair company at a wonderful price.
quotes.notes: Buffett crediting Munger for pushing him from cigar-butt bargains toward quality businesses.

quotes.content: Price is what you pay; value is what you get.
quotes.source: Quoted by Warren Buffett, Berkshire Hathaway shareholder letter (2008)
quotes.notes: Buffett attributes this maxim to his teacher Ben Graham in the 2008 letter.

quotes.content: Confronted with a like challenge to distill the secret of sound investment into three words, we venture the motto, MARGIN OF SAFETY.
quotes.notes: Graham's summation of his central investing principle in the book's final chapter.

quotes.content: The investor's chief problem—and even his worst enemy—is likely to be himself.
quotes.notes: On temperament, not intellect, as the true test of the investor.

quotes.content: In the short run, the market is a voting machine but in the long run, it is a weighing machine.
quotes.notes: Unverified: this tidy wording is not found verbatim in Security Analysis or The Intelligent Investor; Buffett recalls Graham saying it in class. Wording/source axis fails.

quotes.content: Be fearful when others are greedy, and be greedy when others are fearful.
quotes.source: "Buy American. I Am.", The New York Times op-ed (2008)
quotes.notes: Buffett's contrarian rule, stated in his op-ed urging stock buying during the financial crisis.

quotes.content: You only find out who is swimming naked when the tide goes out.
quotes.notes: On hidden risks exposed when markets turn, written in the wake of insurance losses.

quotes.content: When we own portions of outstanding businesses with outstanding managements, our favorite holding period is forever.
quotes.notes: The full sentence behind the often-truncated 'favorite holding period is forever.'

quotes.content: Whether we're talking about socks or stocks, I like buying quality merchandise when it is marked down.
quotes.notes: Written after the 2008 crash on treating market declines as sales.

quotes.content: Rule No. 1: Never lose money. Rule No. 2: Never forget rule No. 1.
quotes.source: Widely attributed; popularized in The Tao of Warren Buffett (2006)
quotes.notes: Unverified: no primary Buffett speech or letter carries this exact phrasing; it traces to a secondary compilation. Source axis fails.

quotes.content: Risk comes from not knowing what you're doing.
quotes.source: Talk at Columbia Business School, reported in the Omaha World-Herald (1994)
quotes.notes: Unverified: reported secondhand and, per Quote Investigator, compressed from non-adjacent phrases. Wording axis fails.

quotes.content: The big money is not in the buying and the selling, but in the waiting.
quotes.notes: Munger on patience as the real engine of compounding returns.

quotes.content: It is remarkable how much long-term advantage people like us have gotten by trying to be consistently not stupid, instead of trying to be very intelligent.
quotes.notes: Munger's inversion principle, later reprinted in Poor Charlie's Almanack.

quotes.content: I constantly see people rise in life who are not the smartest, sometimes not even the most diligent, but they are learning machines. They go to bed every night a little wiser than they were when they got up.
quotes.notes: Munger on continuous learning as a compounding advantage over a long career.

quotes.content: Don't look for the needle in the haystack. Just buy the haystack!
quotes.source: The Little Book of Common Sense Investing (2007)
quotes.notes: The Vanguard founder's case for owning the whole market via low-cost index funds.

quotes.content: Time is your friend; impulse is your enemy.
quotes.source: The Little Book of Common Sense Investing (2007)
quotes.notes: Bogle on patience and the cost of emotional trading.

quotes.content: Know what you own, and know why you own it.
quotes.source: Attributed to One Up on Wall Street (1989)
quotes.notes: Unverified: universally attributed to Lynch's book but confirmed here only via quote aggregators, not primary text. Source axis fails.

quotes.content: Investing without research is like playing stud poker and never looking at the cards.
quotes.source: Attributed to One Up on Wall Street (1989)
quotes.notes: Unverified: attributed to Lynch's book but confirmed here only via aggregators, not primary text. Source axis fails.

quotes.content: Pain plus reflection equals progress.
quotes.notes: Dalio's core formula, rendered in the book as 'Pain + Reflection = Progress.'

quotes.content: He who lives by the crystal ball is destined to eat ground glass.
quotes.notes: A market adage Dalio says he learned young and cites in Principles; he credits it as an existing saying.

quotes.content: Doing well with money has a little to do with how smart you are and a lot to do with how you behave.
quotes.notes: The book's central thesis, from its introduction.

quotes.content: Wealth is what you don't see. Wealth is financial assets that haven't yet been converted into the stuff you see.
quotes.notes: Housel distinguishing spent income (things) from unspent wealth (options).

quotes.content: Saving is the gap between your ego and your income.
quotes.notes: On restraint rather than income as the driver of savings.

quotes.content: A budget is people telling their money where to go instead of wondering where it went.
quotes.source: Attributed to The Total Money Makeover (2003)
quotes.notes: Unverified: signature Ramsey line but confirmed here only via aggregators, not primary text; wording also circulates in variant forms. Source axis fails.

quotes.content: Beware of little expenses; a small leak will sink a great ship.
quotes.notes: From Franklin's Poor Richard essay compiling his thrift maxims.

quotes.content: Early to bed and early to rise, makes a man healthy, wealthy, and wise.
quotes.notes: A proverb Franklin popularized in the 1735 almanac; earlier versions predate him.

quotes.content: If you would know the value of money, go and try to borrow some; for he that goes a borrowing goes a sorrowing.
quotes.notes: Franklin on the true cost of debt.

quotes.content: Lost time is never found again.
quotes.notes: Franklin quoting Poor Richard on the irrecoverable cost of wasted time.

quotes.content: The first thing is character. Before money or anything else. Money cannot buy it.
quotes.source: Testimony before the Pujo Committee, U.S. House of Representatives (1912)
quotes.notes: Morgan's sworn reply on what credit rests upon; from the Money Trust investigation transcript.

quotes.content: Gold is money. Everything else is credit.
quotes.notes: Unverified: the transcript reads 'The money is gold, and nothing else'; this tidy paraphrase is not the verbatim testimony. Wording axis fails.

quotes.content: Experience is what you got when you didn't get what you wanted.
quotes.source: The Most Important Thing Illuminated: Uncommon Sense for the Thoughtful Investor (2013)
quotes.notes: Marks's maxim from the book's chapters on risk, on why hard markets teach the lessons easy ones cannot.

quotes.content: The three most harmful addictions are heroin, carbohydrates, and a monthly salary.
quotes.source: The Bed of Procrustes: Philosophical and Practical Aphorisms (2010)
quotes.notes: Often misattributed to Taleb's later Skin in the Game; the aphorism originates in this 2010 collection.

quotes.content: Value investing is at its core the marriage of a contrarian streak and a calculator.
quotes.source: Margin of Safety: Risk-Averse Value Investing Strategies for the Thoughtful Investor (1991)
quotes.notes: Klarman's summary of the temperament value investing demands; the long-out-of-print book now sells for thousands of dollars used.
